subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the vendor risk tiering methodology and classification criteria, confirming it addresses service criticality, data access (client PII, market data), and capital market system dependencies (trading platforms, exchange connectivity, clearing interfaces).
2. Verify that tiering criteria differentiate between vendors with access to client data under PDPA, vendors providing market data feeds, and vendors supporting non-critical functions.
3. Review the complete third-party register and confirm all technology service providers are classified by risk tier.
4. Determine whether the tiering methodology has been reviewed and approved within the last 12 months.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Vendor risk tiering methodology document; Third-party technology service provider register with tier classifications; Board or management approval of tiering criteria; Criteria mapping for capital market-specific vendor categories

subProcedures.subProcedure: Due diligence process for critical vendors
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the due diligence procedures for onboarding new third-party technology providers and confirm they define assessment depth by risk tier.
2. Select a sample of 3 critical-tier vendors (including at least one market data provider or exchange connectivity provider) and review the due diligence assessments conducted at onboarding.
3. Verify that due diligence for critical vendors includes financial stability checks, information security capability assessment, regulatory compliance status, and business continuity arrangements.
4. Confirm that due diligence findings were formally accepted or resulted in documented risk treatment before vendor engagement.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Vendor due diligence procedures; Due diligence assessment reports for sampled vendors; Financial stability and credit checks; Risk acceptance or treatment records for identified findings

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. For the same sample of 3 critical-tier vendors, obtain evidence of security posture assessment conducted (security questionnaires, independent security reviews, or assurance reports).
2. Verify that vendors with access to client data or market systems have provided current SOC 2 Type II reports, ISO 27001 certificates, or equivalent independent assurance.
3. Review how assurance report findings (particularly complementary user entity controls) are assessed for relevance to the entity's risk profile.
4. Confirm that vendors providing trading platform services or exchange connectivity have undergone security assessments covering system availability and data integrity.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Completed vendor security questionnaires; SOC 2 Type II reports or ISO 27001 certificates for critical vendors; Security assessment reports for trading system vendors; Assurance report gap analysis or CUEC mapping

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain evidence of ongoing risk monitoring activities for critical vendors over the last 12 months, including periodic risk reviews, threat intelligence related to vendors, and financial health monitoring.
2. Review how changes in vendor risk profile (e.g., data breaches, financial distress, regulatory action) are detected and escalated.
3. Verify that monitoring frequency is proportionate to risk tier — at minimum quarterly for critical-tier vendors providing market infrastructure services.
4. Interview vendor risk management staff to understand how monitoring findings trigger reassessment or remediation.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Vendor risk monitoring reports for last 12 months; Vendor risk alert or notification records; Escalation records for changes in vendor risk profile; Evidence of periodic financial health checks for critical vendors

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the schedule of annual vendor reassessments and verify all critical-tier vendors were reassessed within the last 12 months.
2. Sample 3 vendor reassessments and confirm the reassessment scope covers security posture, service delivery performance, compliance status, and any changes in sub-contracting arrangements.
3. Verify that reassessment results are compared against prior year to identify trends or deterioration in vendor risk posture.
4. Confirm that reassessment findings are reported to senior management and that remediation actions are tracked to closure.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Annual vendor reassessment schedule and completion records; Reassessment reports for sampled vendors; Year-over-year trend analysis; Management reporting of reassessment findings with remediation tracking

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the outsourcing policy and verify it is aligned with SC-GL/6-2023 requirements, including definitions of material outsourcing, risk assessment requirements, and governance structures.
2. Verify the policy defines what constitutes material outsourcing for capital market intermediaries (e.g., outsourcing of trading operations, clearing/settlement functions, client data processing, or IT infrastructure supporting market operations).
3. Confirm the policy mandates board approval for material outsourcing arrangements, SC notification where required, and periodic review of all outsourcing arrangements.
4. Determine that the policy was reviewed and approved by the board within the last 12 months and that staff with outsourcing management responsibilities have been made aware of the policy.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Outsourcing policy document; Board approval records for the policy; SC-GL/6-2023 requirement mapping against policy provisions; Policy distribution and acknowledgement records

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the register of all material outsourcing arrangements and confirm each arrangement has documented board approval.
2. Review board papers for 2 material outsourcing arrangements and verify the board was provided with adequate information including risk assessment results, proposed controls and monitoring mechanisms, and impact on capital market operations.
3. Verify that SC notification requirements for material outsourcing have been met where applicable, including outsourcing of critical market infrastructure functions.
4. Confirm that the board has delegated day-to-day outsourcing oversight to an appropriate management committee with defined terms of reference.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Material outsourcing register; Board meeting minutes with outsourcing approval decisions; Board papers with risk assessments and control proposals; SC notification records for material outsourcing; Management committee terms of reference for outsourcing oversight

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Select 3 material outsourcing contracts (including at least one involving client data processing and one involving trading or market infrastructure services) and inspect for mandatory contractual provisions.
2. Verify each contract includes: defined SLAs with measurable performance targets, right-to-audit clauses permitting the entity and SC to audit the service provider, data protection obligations aligned with PDPA, business continuity and disaster recovery commitments, termination and exit provisions, and regulatory access provisions.
3. Confirm that contracts specify data handling obligations including restrictions on data location, use, and disclosure for client information and market data.
4. Determine whether contract templates have been reviewed by legal counsel with knowledge of SC-GL/6-2023 requirements.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Sample outsourcing contracts (3 material arrangements); Contract review checklist against SC-GL/6-2023 mandatory provisions; Legal counsel review records; Data protection schedules or annexes within contracts

subProcedures.subProcedure: Outsourcing risk reporting to the board
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain quarterly outsourcing risk reports to the board for the last 4 quarters and verify reporting cadence is maintained.
2. Review report content and confirm it covers: vendor performance against SLAs, significant incidents involving outsourced services, changes in vendor risk profile, compliance with contractual obligations, and any new or terminated outsourcing arrangements.
3. Verify that board actions or decisions arising from outsourcing risk reports are documented and tracked.
4. Interview the COO or outsourcing governance lead to understand how the reporting captures risks from outsourced capital market functions (e.g., trade execution, settlement, custody).
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Quarterly outsourcing risk reports for last 4 quarters; Board meeting minutes showing discussion of outsourcing reports; Action tracking records from board outsourcing decisions; Reporting templates and data sources

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain records of SC notifications submitted for material outsourcing arrangements and verify completeness against the material outsourcing register.
2. Review notification content to confirm it includes the information required by SC-GL/6-2023, including description of outsourced function, risk assessment summary, and controls implemented.
3. Verify that SC responses or conditions (if any) have been addressed and tracked to closure.
4. Determine whether the entity maintains a tracking mechanism for SC notification requirements across all material outsourcing arrangements.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: SC notification records for material outsourcing; Material outsourcing register cross-referenced to notification status; SC response and condition tracking records; Notification workflow documentation

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the SLA monitoring framework and verify it defines how vendor performance is measured, reported, and escalated for all outsourced technology services.
2. Verify that SLA metrics are defined for critical capital market vendor categories: market data providers (latency, accuracy, availability), trading platform vendors (uptime, order execution performance), and exchange connectivity providers (connection availability, failover times).
3. Confirm that SLA measurement is based on independent monitoring or verified vendor reporting rather than unverified vendor self-reporting alone.
4. Review whether SLA thresholds and targets have been calibrated against capital market operational requirements (e.g., trading hours availability, settlement window performance).
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: SLA monitoring framework document; Defined SLA metrics for each critical vendor category; Independent monitoring tool configurations or reports; SLA threshold calibration records aligned to market operational requirements

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain monthly performance reports for 3 critical vendors from the last quarter, including at least one market data provider or exchange connectivity provider.
2. Verify SLA metrics are tracked against agreed targets, deviations are highlighted, and trends are analysed.
3. Review how performance data is aggregated and presented to management, including dashboards or summary reports.
4. Confirm that performance reports differentiate between minor SLA misses and material performance degradation affecting capital market operations.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Monthly vendor performance reports for 3 critical vendors (last quarter); SLA compliance dashboards or scorecards; Performance trend analysis; Management summary reports on vendor performance

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the escalation procedures for SLA breaches and verify they define escalation tiers, timelines, and responsible parties.
2. Select 2 instances of SLA breaches from the last 12 months and trace the escalation process to verify timely notification, root cause analysis, and remediation actions.
3. Verify that service credits or contractual penalties were assessed and applied where applicable.
4. Confirm that persistent underperformance triggers formal remediation plans or consideration of vendor replacement, particularly for vendors supporting trading or settlement operations.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: SLA breach escalation procedures; SLA breach records with escalation trail for sampled incidents; Root cause analysis and remediation action records; Service credit or penalty application records; Remediation plans for persistent underperformance

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain records of periodic vendor governance meetings or relationship reviews for 3 critical vendors from the last 12 months.
2. Review meeting minutes or action logs and verify discussions covered: service delivery performance, upcoming changes or enhancements, risk and compliance matters, and strategic alignment.
3. Confirm that action items from governance meetings are tracked to closure.
4. Interview the service management team to understand how vendor relationship reviews feed into annual reassessment and contract renewal decisions for capital market service providers.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Vendor governance meeting minutes or records for 3 critical vendors; Action item tracking logs; Meeting attendance records; Relationship review summary reports

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the fourth-party register or sub-contractor disclosure records for all critical service providers and verify completeness against contractual disclosure requirements.
2. Verify that critical vendors (market data providers, trading platform vendors, exchange connectivity providers) have disclosed all sub-contractors that access client data or market systems.
3. Confirm that sub-contractor disclosures include the nature of services provided, data access granted, and geographic location of service delivery.
4. Review whether the entity has a process to receive and act on notifications of sub-contractor changes from critical vendors.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Fourth-party register or sub-contractor disclosure records; Contractual disclosure requirement clauses; Sub-contractor change notification records; Process documentation for managing sub-contractor disclosures

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Select 2 critical supply chains (e.g., trading platform vendor and its infrastructure sub-contractors; market data provider and its data source aggregators) and review fourth-party risk assessments conducted.
2. Verify that assessments evaluate the impact of fourth-party failure on capital market operations, including trading continuity, settlement processing, and client data protection.
3. Confirm that risk assessments consider geographic and jurisdictional risks for fourth parties operating outside Malaysia, particularly where client data under PDPA may be accessed.
4. Determine whether fourth-party risk assessment results are integrated into the overall vendor risk profile for the primary service provider.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Fourth-party risk assessment reports for 2 critical supply chains; Supply chain mapping diagrams; Jurisdictional risk analysis for offshore fourth parties; Integrated vendor risk profiles reflecting fourth-party risks

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Review contracts with 3 critical vendors and verify they include provisions requiring prior approval or notification before engaging new sub-contractors for services involving client data or market systems.
2. Confirm contracts include flow-down requirements ensuring sub-contractors meet the same security, data protection, and confidentiality obligations as the primary vendor.
3. Verify that contracts reserve the entity's right (and the SC's right) to audit sub-contractors providing critical services.
4. Determine whether contracts include provisions for termination if sub-contracting arrangements materially change the risk profile.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Sub-contracting clauses in 3 critical vendor contracts; Flow-down obligation provisions; Audit right provisions extending to sub-contractors; Termination trigger clauses related to sub-contracting changes

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain or construct a supply chain dependency map for critical capital market services and identify where multiple vendors depend on the same fourth party (e.g., multiple vendors using the same CSP, or common market data aggregator).
2. Verify that concentration risks have been formally assessed, particularly for shared infrastructure dependencies affecting trading, clearing, or settlement.
3. Review whether mitigating actions have been identified and implemented for material concentration risks (e.g., alternative providers, multi-cloud arrangements, diversified data feeds).
4. Interview vendor risk management staff to understand how fourth-party concentration risks are monitored on an ongoing basis.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Supply chain dependency map or concentration risk analysis; Concentration risk assessment report; Mitigation action plans for identified concentration risks; Monitoring reports for ongoing concentration risk tracking

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the cloud risk assessment framework or methodology and verify it addresses shared responsibility, multi-tenancy, data residency, vendor lock-in, and regulatory requirements specific to Malaysian capital market data.
2. Verify the framework mandates risk assessment before any new cloud adoption involving client data (subject to PDPA), market data, or capital market system workloads.
3. Confirm the framework defines risk acceptance criteria and approval authorities for cloud deployments at different risk levels.
4. Determine whether the framework has been reviewed within the last 12 months and approved by senior management or the board.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud risk assessment framework or methodology document; Framework approval records; Risk acceptance criteria and approval authority matrix; Framework review and update records

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the shared responsibility matrices for all current CSP engagements involving capital market systems or client data.
2. Verify each matrix clearly delineates security responsibilities between the entity and the CSP across infrastructure, platform, application, and data layers.
3. Sample 2 CSP engagements and confirm the entity has implemented its side of the shared responsibility obligations (e.g., IAM configuration, data encryption, network security controls).
4. Review whether shared responsibility matrices are reviewed and updated when CSPs change their service models or introduce new features.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Shared responsibility matrices for each CSP engagement; Evidence of entity-side control implementation for sampled engagements; CSP service model change tracking records; Responsibility matrix review and update log

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the data residency assessment for cloud-hosted capital market data and verify it identifies where client information (subject to PDPA) and market data are physically stored and processed.
2. Verify that data residency requirements have been assessed against PDPA cross-border transfer provisions and SC-GL/6-2023 expectations for data sovereignty.
3. Confirm that CSP contracts specify permitted data storage jurisdictions and that technical controls enforce residency restrictions (e.g., region-locked deployments).
4. Review whether the entity has assessed the legal and regulatory implications of data being accessible from jurisdictions outside Malaysia, even if stored domestically.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Data residency assessment report; PDPA cross-border transfer compliance analysis; CSP contractual data residency provisions; Technical configuration evidence for region-locked deployments; Legal opinion on jurisdictional data access risks

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain current CSP certifications and independent assurance reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, CSA STAR) for all CSPs hosting capital market systems or client data.
2. Verify that assurance reports are current (issued within the last 12 months) and that the scope covers the specific services and regions used by the entity.
3. Review complementary user entity controls (CUECs) identified in SOC 2 reports and verify the entity has implemented them.
4. Confirm that any qualifications, exceptions, or findings in assurance reports have been assessed for impact on the entity's risk profile and addressed where necessary.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: CSP SOC 2 Type II reports; ISO 27001 certificates for CSPs; CSA STAR certification records; CUEC implementation evidence; Gap analysis for assurance report findings or qualifications

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain evidence of cloud security posture assessments conducted for environments hosting capital market workloads, including configuration reviews and compliance checks.
2. Verify that cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools or processes are in place to detect misconfigurations, excessive permissions, and non-compliant resource deployments.
3. Sample 2 cloud environments and review security posture assessment results, including findings, severity ratings, and remediation status.
4. Confirm that critical and high-severity cloud security findings are remediated within defined SLAs and that residual risks are formally accepted by appropriate management.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud security posture assessment reports; CSPM tool configuration and output; Remediation tracking records for cloud security findings; Risk acceptance records for residual cloud security risks

subProcedures.subProcedure: Cloud risk assessment completion for current engagements
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain an inventory of all current cloud engagements involving client data or market systems and cross-reference against completed cloud risk assessments.
2. Verify that each engagement had a risk assessment completed before cloud adoption, not retrospectively.
3. Review 2 completed cloud risk assessments and confirm they address all framework requirements including shared responsibility, data residency, multi-tenancy risks, and capital market-specific considerations.
4. Determine whether cloud risk assessments are refreshed periodically (at minimum annually) or when material changes occur to the cloud deployment.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud engagement inventory; Completed cloud risk assessments for all engagements; Risk assessment completion dates versus cloud adoption dates; Periodic reassessment records

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain the inventory of data types stored in cloud environments and verify classification labels are applied consistent with the entity's data classification scheme.
2. Verify that classification distinguishes between client PII (subject to PDPA), market-sensitive data, trading data, and general corporate data, with protection levels defined for each.
3. Sample 2 cloud environments and confirm that data classification labels are technically enforced (e.g., resource tagging, metadata labels, automated classification tools).
4. Review whether data classification in cloud is periodically reviewed to capture new data types or changes in sensitivity as capital market operations evolve.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud data inventory with classification labels; Data classification scheme applied to cloud data types; Resource tagging or metadata label configurations for sampled environments; Classification review records

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Verify encryption controls for sensitive data in cloud environments: inspect encryption configuration for data at rest and data in transit for all cloud environments hosting client data or market data.
2. Confirm that customer-managed keys (BYOK or HYOK) are used for encrypting client PII and market-sensitive data, rather than relying solely on CSP-managed keys.
3. Review key management procedures including key generation, rotation schedules, access controls on key management systems, and key lifecycle management.
4. Determine whether encryption key management has been assessed for compliance with PDPA data protection requirements and whether key escrow or recovery procedures are documented.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Encryption configuration evidence for cloud environments (at-rest and in-transit); Customer-managed key (BYOK) deployment records; Key management procedure documentation; Key rotation schedules and execution logs; Key access control configurations

subProcedures.subProcedure: Cloud access controls and privileged access
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Review IAM policies and access control configurations for cloud environments hosting client data, including role definitions, least-privilege enforcement, and segregation of duties.
2. Verify that multi-factor authentication is enforced for all access to cloud environments containing client PII or market data, particularly for privileged and administrative accounts.
3. Obtain the privileged access register for cloud environments and verify that privileged access is limited, justified, reviewed at least quarterly, and subject to enhanced monitoring.
4. Confirm that access provisioning and de-provisioning processes are timely and that access reviews have been completed within the last quarter.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: IAM policy configurations for cloud environments; MFA enforcement evidence; Privileged access register with justification records; Quarterly access review records; Access provisioning and de-provisioning logs

subProcedures.subProcedure: Cloud data access monitoring and anomaly detection
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Review cloud access logs and audit trails for the last quarter to verify that data access monitoring is active for environments hosting client data and market data.
2. Verify that logging covers all access to sensitive data including administrative actions, data exports, bulk queries, and API access to client information.
3. Confirm that anomalous access patterns (e.g., unusual data downloads, access from unexpected locations, off-hours privileged access) are detected and investigated.
4. Sample 2 anomaly alerts or investigations from the last 6 months and verify they were responded to within defined SLAs, with documented investigation outcomes.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud access logs and audit trail configurations; Data access monitoring dashboard or reports; Anomaly detection rule configurations; Investigation records for sampled anomaly alerts; Alert response SLA compliance reports

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Inspect cloud deployment configurations for environments hosting Malaysian capital market client data and verify that data residency restrictions are technically enforced (e.g., region-locked resource groups, geo-fencing policies).
2. Verify that data residency controls prevent data replication, backup, or processing in non-approved jurisdictions.
3. Review CSP data residency commitment documentation and confirm it aligns with PDPA cross-border data transfer requirements.
4. Confirm that data residency compliance is monitored on an ongoing basis and that any residency violations are detected and escalated promptly.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud deployment region configurations; Geo-fencing or region-lock policy settings; CSP data residency commitment documentation; Data residency monitoring reports; Residency violation detection and escalation records

subProcedures.subProcedure: Exit plan documentation for material cloud deployments
subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain documented exit plans for all material cloud deployments hosting capital market systems or client data and verify they exist for each material engagement.
2. Verify each exit plan covers: data extraction procedures, service migration approach, timeline estimates and cost projections, communication plan for stakeholders, and continuity of capital market operations during transition.
3. Confirm that exit plans address the handling of client data under PDPA during and after migration, including secure deletion from the departing CSP.
4. Determine whether exit plans have been reviewed and updated within the last 12 months and approved by the CTO or equivalent.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud exit plans for all material deployments; Exit plan approval records; Timeline and cost estimates for each exit scenario; PDPA data handling procedures during exit; Exit plan review and update log

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Review cloud workload designs for portability considerations, including use of open standards, containerisation, and avoidance of proprietary lock-in for critical capital market services.
2. Verify that data export formats have been defined and tested for all material data categories (client records, trading data, market data, configuration data) ensuring data can be extracted in usable, non-proprietary formats.
3. Confirm that CSP contracts include provisions for data retrieval timelines, format commitments, and assistance obligations upon exit.
4. Review contractual provisions for data deletion by the CSP post-termination and verify they align with PDPA data retention and destruction requirements.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Cloud architecture documentation showing portability considerations; Data export format specifications and test results; CSP contractual exit assistance provisions; Data deletion obligation clauses; Vendor lock-in assessment for critical services

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Obtain records of data extraction and migration testing conducted for material cloud deployments, verifying tests were performed within the last 12 months.
2. Verify that migration tests include end-to-end data extraction, integrity validation (record counts, checksums), and confirmation that all data categories can be retrieved in agreed formats.
3. Review test results including time to extract, data completeness verification, and any issues encountered and their resolution.
4. Confirm that migration testing for capital market data covers trading records, client data, and regulatory reporting data to ensure continuity of SC and Bursa Malaysia reporting obligations.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Data migration test plans and execution records; Test results with data integrity validation (checksums, record counts); Time-to-extract measurements; Issue logs and resolution records from migration tests; Coverage confirmation for regulatory reporting data

subProcedures.assessmentProcedures:
1. Interview the CTO or cloud architecture team to understand identified alternative CSPs or on-premises fallback options for each critical cloud service.
2. Verify that alternative providers have been assessed for capability to host capital market workloads, including compliance with data residency requirements, availability of required certifications, and ability to meet performance SLAs.
3. Review whether transition timelines and effort estimates have been developed for migrating to alternative providers.
4. Confirm that contingency arrangements are documented and integrated into the entity's business continuity planning for scenarios involving CSP failure or sudden termination.
subProcedures.expectedEvidence: Alternative provider assessment records; Capability comparison matrix for alternative CSPs; Transition timeline and effort estimates; BCP integration documentation for cloud contingency scenarios; CTO or cloud architecture team interview notes
